questions.stem: According to Fowler's definition, what is refactoring?

options.text: A change made to the internal structure of software to make it easier to understand and cheaper to modify, without changing observable behaviour
A change that adds new features while keeping the existing tests green
An optimisation that improves runtime performance by restructuring algorithms
A rewrite of a module from scratch to remove accumulated technical debt

questions.explanation: Fowler defines refactoring as a change to the internal structure to make software easier to understand and cheaper to modify, with observable behaviour unchanged. B is wrong because refactoring adds no functionality. C confuses refactoring with performance tuning; the goal is understandability/modifiability, not speed. D is a rewrite, not a behaviour-preserving restructuring. (Refactoring1 p.3)

questions.stem: A developer restructures a method's internals and, in the process, a previously-correct output value becomes different. Why is this NOT a refactoring?

options.text: Because the change was not verified by automated tests
Because refactoring is only allowed during the Postfactoring phase
Because the observable behaviour changed, so by definition it is a modification, not a refactoring
Because refactoring may only be applied to methods shorter than 15 lines

questions.explanation: By definition refactoring preserves observable behaviour; if an output changes it is a functional modification, not a refactoring. D invents a length rule that does not gate the definition. A is good practice but a missing test does not change the definition. B is false; refactoring happens in both Prefactoring and Postfactoring. (Refactoring1 p.3)

questions.stem: Which pair correctly states the two goals of refactoring named in Fowler's definition?

options.text: Faster to execute and smaller in memory
Easier to understand and cheaper to modify

questions.explanation: Fowler's definition names two human-facing goals: easier to understand and cheaper to modify. A is performance, not a refactoring goal. B adds features/fixes bugs which are not refactoring's defining goals. C names documentation/portability which are not in the definition. (Refactoring1 p.3)

questions.stem: Kerievsky's phrase 'the algebra of refactoring' most directly refers to which idea?

options.text: Computing man-months from lines of code
Measuring fan-in to assess module coupling
Solving design problems through sequences of small behaviour-preserving steps
Calculating cyclomatic complexity from branch points

questions.explanation: Kerievsky calls patterns the 'word problems' and refactoring the 'algebra' — solving design problems via behaviour-preserving steps. D, A, and B are SIG/BetterCode measurement formulas, a different deck's concern, not the algebra metaphor. (HighLevelRefactoring p.10)

questions.stem: Fowler's 'Refactor When You Add Function' trigger maps to which change-process phase in this course?
questions.explanation: Cleaning up so a new feature fits is refactoring before the functional change — Prefactoring. Postfactoring is the after-cleanup. Actualization is where the new behaviour is actually added. Concept Location is finding where to change, an earlier phase. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Fowler's 'Refactor As You Do a Code Review' trigger is described in the course as territory for which phase?
questions.explanation: A code review surfaces smells that are fixed after the change has been made — Postfactoring territory. Prefactoring is the before-cleanup tied to 'add function'. Concept Location and Impact Analysis are earlier, non-refactoring phases. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: What single property distinguishes Prefactoring from Postfactoring?

options.text: Which refactoring catalog they may draw from
Their position relative to the functional change (Actualization)

questions.explanation: Both are behaviour-preserving and use the same catalog and metrics; the only difference is position relative to Actualization (before vs after). C is false — tests support both. D is false — both preserve behaviour. A is false — the same Fowler/Kerievsky catalog applies to both. (course)

questions.stem: Which phase, unlike Prefactoring and Postfactoring, actually DOES change observable behaviour?
questions.explanation: Actualization is where new functionality is injected, so it changes observable behaviour. Prefactoring and Postfactoring are behaviour-preserving. Concept Location, Impact Analysis, and Initiation are analysis/planning phases that change no code behaviour. (course)

questions.stem: In change-process terms, Prefactoring maps to which Fowler trigger, and Postfactoring to which guideline?

options.text: Prefactoring -> Rule of Three; Postfactoring -> Guideline 1 (15 LOC)
Prefactoring -> Guideline 9 (Automated Tests); Postfactoring -> 'Refactor to fix a bug'
Prefactoring -> Guideline 4 (4 params); Postfactoring -> Rule of Three
Prefactoring -> 'Refactor When You Add Function'; Postfactoring -> BetterCode Guideline 10 'leave no smells behind'

questions.explanation: Prefactoring corresponds to Fowler's 'Refactor When You Add Function'; Postfactoring corresponds to BetterCode G10 'Write Clean Code / leave no smells behind'. The other pairings mismatch the mapping the course draws. (course; Refactoring1 p.5; BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT one of Fowler's seven refactoring categories?
questions.explanation: The seven categories are Composing Methods, Moving Features, Organizing Data, Simplifying Conditionals, Making Method Calls Simpler, Dealing with Generalization, Big Refactorings. 'Replacing Conditional Dispatchers' is not a category (it echoes a Kerievsky refactoring name). The others are genuine categories. (Refactoring1 p.9)

questions.stem: Hide Delegate and Remove Middle Man are inverse refactorings. How do you choose between them?

options.text: Choose based on which has the lower [Fowler99] page number
By which smell dominates — too much delegation (Middle Man) -> Remove Middle Man; chains/coupling -> Hide Delegate
Always prefer Hide Delegate because hiding is safer
Always prefer Remove Middle Man because fewer methods is simpler

questions.explanation: These inverses pull opposite ways; pick by the dominant smell — Middle Man (over-delegation) calls for Remove Middle Man, Message Chains/coupling call for Hide Delegate. C and D are false absolutes. A is the page-number trap (book pages are not selection criteria). (Refactoring1 p.19-20)

questions.stem: Whose book is the source of the high-level / 'Refactoring to Patterns' material?
questions.explanation: The high-level/composite refactoring deck is Kerievsky's 'Refactoring to Patterns' [Ker05]. Fowler wrote the low-level catalog, SIG/Visser wrote 'Building Maintainable Software', and the Gang of Four wrote 'Design Patterns'. (HighLevelRefactoring p.2)

questions.stem: In Kerievsky's metaphor 'Design patterns are the word problems of the programming world; refactoring is its ___', which word fills the blank?
questions.explanation: Kerievsky's metaphor: patterns are the word problems, refactoring is the algebra (you solve a design problem via a sequence of behaviour-preserving moves). The other words do not complete the slide's phrase. (HighLevelRefactoring p.9)

questions.stem: In Kerievsky's catalog, which refactoring addresses the Switch Statements smell by introducing a Command?
questions.explanation: Replace Conditional Dispatcher with Command targets Switch Statements (and Long/Large smells) via the Command pattern. Replace Conditional Logic with Strategy uses Strategy; Form Template Method targets duplication; Unify Interfaces with Adapter targets differing interfaces. (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: Which Kerievsky-only smell (not in Fowler's list) is fixed by 'Replace Implicit Language with Interpreter'?
questions.explanation: Combinatorial Explosion is a Kerievsky-only smell addressed by Replace Implicit Language with Interpreter. Duplicated Code, Large Class, and Primitive Obsession are Fowler smells (marked [F]) with other Kerievsky fixes. (HighLevelRefactoring p.12)

questions.stem: Why is Kerievsky's 'refactoring to patterns' deck especially applicable to JHotDraw?

options.text: JHotDraw has no design patterns, so every refactoring adds one
JHotDraw is written procedurally, so only Convert Procedural Design applies
JHotDraw forbids inheritance, so only delegation refactorings apply
JHotDraw is a GoF design-pattern showcase, so its patterns are exactly the targets of Kerievsky's refactorings

questions.explanation: JHotDraw heavily uses GoF patterns (Factory Method, Strategy, Composite, Observer, Decorator, etc.), which are precisely what Kerievsky's refactorings target. A/B/C mischaracterise JHotDraw, which is rich in patterns and inheritance. (course)

questions.stem: Which Big Refactoring corresponds to JHotDraw's MVC-style split between the Drawing/Figure domain model and the DrawingView/GUI presentation?
questions.explanation: Separate Domain from Presentation maps onto JHotDraw's domain (Drawing/Figure) vs presentation (DrawingView/GUI) layering. The others address inheritance, conditionals, and procedural code. (Refactoring1 p.56)

questions.stem: Which Fowler refactoring (Organizing Data) supports the GUI/domain synchronisation seen in JHotDraw's MVC design?
questions.explanation: Duplicate Observed Data copies GUI-control data into a domain object with an observer to keep them in sync — exactly the JHotDraw domain/presentation separation. The other Organizing Data refactorings serve different ends. (Refactoring1 p.27)

questions.stem: On what kind of branch does the lab ask you to apply the refactorings?
questions.explanation: The lab's workflow uses a feature branch for the refactoring work. Working directly on main, a detached HEAD, or a release tag are not the prescribed workflow. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: Which catalog of refactorings does the lab ask you to APPLY to the JHotDraw smells?
questions.explanation: The lab directs you to apply suitable Kerievsky [Ker05] refactoring patterns to remove the smells. A/B/C mischaracterise the lab task. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: Kerievsky lists multiple refactorings for Long Method. Which one moves accumulation logic into a Collecting Parameter?
questions.explanation: Move Accumulation to Collecting Parameter is the named refactoring for accumulation in a Long Method. Compose Method composes small steps, Replace Conditional Dispatcher with Command uses Command, Move Accumulation to Visitor uses Visitor. (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: In Kerievsky's catalog, Primitive Obsession can be refactored toward which pattern via 'Replace Type Code with Class'?

options.text: A dedicated class replacing the primitive type code

questions.explanation: Replace Type Code with Class introduces a class to replace primitive type codes — one of Kerievsky's Primitive Obsession fixes. Singleton, Observer, and Template Method are reached by other refactorings. (HighLevelRefactoring p.13)

questions.stem: While finishing a task you notice a method littered with comments that merely re-explain confusing code. Applying the Comments-smell guidance, what is the correct fix?

options.text: Delete every comment in the file
Extract Method and give it a self-documenting name
Add more comments until it is clear
Leave it; comments are always good

questions.explanation: The Comments smell means comments compensate for unclear code; the fix is Extract Method plus a good name so the comment becomes unnecessary, not blanket deletion. (Refactoring1 p.8)

questions.stem: Your team keeps copy-pasting a 12-line validation block. You decide to act on the Rule of Three. What does that rule tell you to do here?

options.text: Wait until a fourth copy appears
Copy it once more, then stop
Refactor now, since this is the third similar occurrence

questions.explanation: The Rule of Three says refactor the third time you do something similar (first do it, second wince, third refactor). Waiting for a fourth or copying again ignores the rule. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of the four SIG levels at which the guidelines operate?
questions.explanation: 'Package-private' is a Java access modifier, not a SIG level. The four levels are Unit, Module, Component, System. (BetterCode p.9)

questions.stem: Which claim about tests and refactoring is FALSE?

options.text: Tests act as a safety net that verifies behaviour preservation
Because refactoring is behaviour-preserving by definition, running tests is unnecessary
Lack of tests makes refactoring unsafe
BetterCode G9 'Automated Tests' supports the refactoring discipline

questions.explanation: Behaviour preservation must be verified, not assumed; tests are the safety net, which is why G9 exists. Claiming tests are unnecessary is wrong practice. (BetterCode p.53)

questions.stem: Guideline 5 (module coupling) and Guideline 6 (component coupling) both target loose coupling. How do they differ in level?

options.text: G5 is at System level; G6 is at Unit level
Both operate at the Unit level
G5 operates at the Module level (fan-in to a file/class); G6 operates at the Component level (hidden-code % across top-level components)
G5 measures parameters; G6 measures lines of code

questions.explanation: G5 is Module-level coupling measured by fan-in; G6 is Component-level coupling measured by the weighted percentage of hidden code. (BetterCode p.5, 34, 39)

questions.stem: What is the most likely consequence of refactoring a tangled module with no automated tests?

options.text: Cyclomatic complexity is guaranteed to drop
You may silently break observable behaviour with no safety net to catch it
The refactoring becomes behaviour-changing by definition

questions.explanation: Without tests, behaviour preservation is unverified, so regressions can slip through unnoticed; that is why G9 (Automated Tests) underpins safe refactoring. (BetterCode p.53)

questions.stem: A method's branch points rise from 4 to 6. What follows under BetterCode G2?

options.text: It now violates G2 (max 4 branch points; CC = 7), and should be simplified

questions.explanation: G2 caps branch points at 4 (CC <= 5). Six branch points give CC = 7, violating G2; split the unit, e.g. via Replace Conditional with Polymorphism. (BetterCode p.13, 16)

questions.stem: Consequence of skipping Postfactoring after Actualization on every task?

options.text: The new feature stops working immediately
Cyclomatic complexity is forced to zero
Smells accumulate and the codebase steadily degrades, raising future change cost

questions.explanation: Postfactoring removes smells the change introduced; skipping it lets debt accumulate, making later changes harder (the opposite of the Boy-Scout rule). (BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: A developer says 'the Rule of Three means I should never duplicate code even once.' Correct them.

options.text: It means duplicate exactly three times always
No: do it the first time, wince and duplicate the second, refactor only the third
It means refactor after the fourth duplication

questions.explanation: The Rule of Three tolerates the first and second occurrence and triggers refactoring on the third similar one. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'refactoring is safe even without tests because it is behaviour-preserving by definition.' What's the flaw?

options.text: Correct: tests are pointless during refactoring
Refactoring changes behaviour, so tests fail anyway
Behaviour preservation is a goal to be verified, not a guarantee; without tests, mistakes go undetected

questions.explanation: The definition states the intended invariant; humans still err, so tests verify it. That's why G9 pairs with the refactoring discipline. (BetterCode p.53)

questions.stem: Which phase comes immediately AFTER Actualization in this lecture's framing?
questions.explanation: Actualization is followed by Postfactoring (clean up smells the new code introduced) before Conclusion/Verification. (BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: Order the lab's workflow steps correctly.

options.text: Run SonarLint -> commit to main -> document -> create branch
Apply refactorings -> create branch -> run SonarLint -> identify smells
Create feature branch -> run SonarLint -> identify smells -> apply Kerievsky refactorings -> document strategy
Document strategy -> create branch -> apply refactorings -> run SonarLint

questions.explanation: The lab sequence is: feature branch, SonarLint, find smells in JHotDraw, apply [Ker05] refactorings, document the strategy. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: Within the change process, which two phases are the 'refactoring bookends' around Actualization?
questions.explanation: Prefactoring (before) and Postfactoring (after) are the two refactoring bookends of Actualization. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Why does the lab require you to work on a feature branch rather than directly on main?

options.text: Because SonarLint only runs on branches
Because refactoring changes behaviour and must be quarantined
Because the GoF patterns require branches
To isolate the refactoring experiment so main stays stable until the work is reviewed/merged

questions.explanation: A feature branch isolates the refactoring work, keeping the mainline stable; the lab prescribes branch -> SonarLint -> refactor -> document. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: A metric report lists a unit: LOC=15, branch points=2, parameters=3, duplication=0. Against G1-G4, how does it fare?

options.text: Passes all four (15<=15 LOC, 2<=4 branch pts, 3<=4 params, no duplication)

questions.explanation: 15 LOC is at the limit (pass), 2 branch points pass G2, 3 params pass G4, zero duplication passes G3 — fully compliant. (BetterCode p.10, 16, 28)

questions.stem: A system reports 9 top-level components, but one component is roughly ten times the size of each of the others. Does it satisfy G7?

options.text: Yes: 9 components is all G7 needs
Yes, because more components is always better
No: G7 requires both ~9 components AND approximately equal size; the size imbalance fails it

questions.explanation: G7 demands both the count (close to 9, 6-12) and roughly equal component sizes; a 10x-larger component violates the balance requirement. (BetterCode p.42)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a motivation BetterCode gives for automated tests (G9)?

options.text: They make code predictable and document it
They help you write better code

questions.explanation: G9's motivations are repeatable testing, efficient development, predictable code, documentation, and writing better code — not runtime speed. (BetterCode p.54)

questions.stem: A system has 4 top-level components of roughly equal size. Does it satisfy G7's count requirement?

options.text: No: the count should be close to 9 (6-12); 4 is too few

questions.explanation: G7 wants the number of top-level components close to 9 (between 6 and 12); 4 is below that range. (BetterCode p.42)

questions.stem: Where do BOTH Prefactoring and Postfactoring sit relative to Concept Location and Impact Analysis?

options.text: Prefactoring before, Postfactoring before Concept Location
After them — you locate and analyse first, then prefactor, actualize, postfactor

questions.explanation: Concept Location and Impact Analysis precede the refactoring bookends; the spine is Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'a module with fan-in 11 is still Excellent.' What is correct?

options.text: No — 11 falls in the 11-20 'Good' band, not the 1-10 'Excellent' band

questions.explanation: The Excellent band is 1-10; fan-in 11 starts the Good band. (BetterCode p.34)

questions.stem: Consequence of accumulating smells without ever refactoring, in design terms?

options.text: The design decays under accumulated changes, raising the cost of future change

questions.explanation: Fowler's first reason to refactor is that without it design decays under change; smells left unattended raise future change cost. (Refactoring1 p.4)

questions.stem: Misconception: 'writing tests slows you down with no design benefit.' What does BetterCode claim?

options.text: Correct: tests have no design benefit
Writing tests makes you write better code and documents it

questions.explanation: G9's motivations include that writing tests makes you write better code and that tests document the code. (BetterCode p.54)

questions.stem: Which phase actually adds the new functionality, distinguishing it from the two refactoring bookends?
questions.explanation: Actualization is the only one of these that changes observable behaviour by adding functionality; Pre/Postfactoring are behaviour-preserving. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: After SonarLint flags smells in the lab, but BEFORE applying refactorings, what should already be done?

options.text: A feature branch should be created to isolate the work
The fix should be merged to main

questions.explanation: The lab creates the feature branch first, then runs SonarLint and applies refactorings before documenting. (RefactLab p.1)

questions.stem: Which guideline pairs 'leave no code smells behind' with the Postfactoring discipline?
questions.explanation: G10 ('leave no smells behind after development work') is literally the Postfactoring discipline. (BetterCode p.57)

questions.stem: In this lecture's framing, which two phases use the SAME catalogs and metrics, differing only in timing?
questions.explanation: Pre- and Postfactoring both draw on the Fowler/Kerievsky catalogs and SIG metrics; only their position around Actualization differs. (Refactoring1 p.5)

questions.stem: Replace Conditional with Polymorphism leans on which OO principle to dispatch behaviour by type?
questions.explanation: The refactoring moves each conditional leg into an overriding subclass method, relying on subtype polymorphism. (Refactoring1 p.34, 255)

questions.stem: A Data Class with only fields and accessors most clearly under-uses which OO idea?
questions.explanation: A Data Class separates behaviour from data, under-using encapsulation (objects should bundle behaviour with state). (Refactoring1 p.8)
